text: The surgical team continues the liver resection, advancing to expose the inferior vena cava.

The inferior vena cava is visible at the bottom of the field, with the caudate lobe completely detached from it. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the liver parenchyma, proceeding step by step.
The surgical field shows bleeding from a short hepatic vein, likely torn at its origin from the inferior vena cava. The surgeon applies compression to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon uses the third arm to compress the left lobe of the liver due to incomplete exposure of the inferior vena cava, managing bleeding from the bottom while accelerating the liver parenchyma transaction.

The surgical field shows clear landmarks including the vena cava at the bottom, the falciform ligament, and the position of the left hepatic vein. Bleeding is visible from the vena cava, but full visibility is hindered until complete removal.
The surgeon completes the division of the left lobe of the liver, finalizing the resection process.

The surgical team places a suture on a small tear in the vena cava, visible at the bottom of the surgical field. The assistant uses suction to clear the area, allowing for better visualization and control of bleeding.
The surgeon has completely exposed the vena cava during a left hepatectomy extended to a portion of segment eight. A selective suture is placed on the vena cava, effectively controlling the bleeding.

The surgeon completes the transaction of the left lobe, revealing the vena cava underneath. Two ducts from the right system are visible, and a long jejunal loop is brought up to the liver for a Roux-en-Y reconstruction following the extended hepatectomy.
